breaking.text: Oil pulls back to $80.45 after Trump administration signals options to address crude price spike
S&P 500 futures up 0.24% to 6,851 as markets brace for February jobs report at 8:30 AM ET
Oil pulls back from $81 after Trump administration signals options to address price spike
S&P 500 futures up 0.24% to 6,851 in overnight trading after Thursday's 0.56% decline
February nonfarm payrolls report due this morning — economists expect just 50,000 jobs added amid geopolitical uncertainty

summary: Markets are cautiously optimistic Friday morning after a volatile week driven by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran that sent oil prices surging over 14% and erased the S&P 500's year-to-date gains. The S&P 500 closed Thursday at 6,830.71 (-0.56%) with the Dow dropping 785 points as WTI crude settled at $81.01, its biggest weekly gain since 2022, though prices eased overnight after Washington signaled intervention. Broadcom delivered a blowout Q1 report after the bell — revenue hit a record $19.3B (+29% YoY) with AI revenue of $8.4B (+106%), and CEO Hock Tan projected $100B in AI chip revenue by 2027. All eyes now turn to the February nonfarm payrolls report due at 8:30 AM, where consensus expects just 50,000 jobs added, potentially depressed by a Kaiser Permanente strike that affected 31,000 workers during the survey week.

summary_history.text: U.S. markets are bracing for Friday's critical February jobs report after a volatile week dominated by the Iran-Strait of Hormuz crisis that has sent oil prices surging nearly 14% in a week. The S&P 500 closed Thursday at 6,830.71, down 0.56%, after failing to breach 6,900 resistance — erasing its 2026 gains. Broadcom provided a bright spot with blowout Q1 earnings, reporting AI revenue of $8.4 billion (up 106% YoY) and projecting $100 billion in AI chip revenue by 2027. Overnight futures point to a modest recovery, with prediction markets giving 65% odds of a higher open, though airline and industrial stocks remain under heavy pressure from energy costs.

Markets are attempting to stabilize Friday after a punishing week driven by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran that began February 28, which has sent oil prices surging 20% and disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. The Dow plunged 785 points (1.6%) on Thursday while the S&P 500 fell 0.56% to 6,830.71, failing to hold the 6,900 resistance level and slipping into negative territory for 2026. After hours, Broadcom delivered a bright spot with record Q1 revenue of $19.3B and AI semiconductor revenue doubling to $8.4B, while announcing a $10B buyback program. All eyes now turn to the February nonfarm payrolls report due Friday morning, with expectations for just 50,000 jobs added and unemployment holding at 4.3%, as traders price in less than one-in-three odds for a June Fed rate cut.

U.S. markets suffered another bruising session on March 5 as the S&P 500 fell roughly 0.8% to close near 6,820 after decisively failing to breach 6,900 resistance, with year-to-date gains now fully erased. The selloff was driven by surging crude oil prices—WTI spiked above $79 per barrel after Iran claimed to have struck an American oil tanker near the Strait of Hormuz—stoking inflation fears and pushing the VIX to 22.08. Eight of eleven S&P sectors ended lower, with defensive rotation accelerating as Microsoft dropped 17% YTD and airlines faced downgrades on fuel cost concerns. Investors now turn to Friday's February nonfarm payrolls report amid a Fed stance shifting toward a data-dependent pause with no rate relief expected before June.

The S&P 500 closed near 6,820 on Thursday, down 0.56%, decisively failing the 6,900 resistance level and erasing all 2026 gains as a triple threat of sticky inflation, hawkish Fed policy, and Middle East escalation rattled investors. Oil prices surged over 8% after Iran's Revolutionary Guard Navy struck an American oil tanker in the Persian Gulf, sending crude above $79 and triggering the commodity's biggest weekly gain since 2022. After the bell, Broadcom delivered a blowout Q1 with record revenue of $19.3 billion and AI semiconductor sales doubling year-over-year to $8.4 billion, sending shares up 5% in extended trading. Markets now brace for Friday's February jobs report, where economists expect a modest 50,000 payroll gain weighed down by a Kaiser Permanente strike that affected 31,000 workers during the BLS survey week.
